title: Shish taouk
subtitle: Lebanese chicken kebab, marinated in yoghurt and lemon then grilled over high heat.
excerpt: Shish taouk is the chicken kebab of the Levantine grill — boneless thigh meat marinated in yoghurt, lemon, garlic, and allspice, then cooked fast over a very hot grill or griddle pan. The yoghurt marinade tenderises and chars. An hour in the fridge is enough; overnight makes it better. Serve in flatbread with garlic sauce and salad.
sourceNotes: Standard Levantine grill preparation, widespread across Lebanese, Syrian, and Palestinian restaurant and home cookery. The yoghurt-and-lemon marinade and allspice seasoning are consistent across twentieth-century Arab culinary documentation. The name translates as 'chicken skewer' from the Turkish şiş (skewer) and Arabic taouk (chicken).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the marinated raw chicken for up to three months. Defrost in the fridge overnight before threading onto skewers and grilling.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. The marinade scales linearly. Cook in two batches over high heat rather than crowding the grill.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate the chicken up to 24 hours ahead. Thread onto skewers just before cooking.

recipeTools.notes: Or a grill rack over a very hot gas flame or charcoal.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dried berry from the Caribbean and Central America with a flavour that combines notes of clove, cinnamon, and nutmeg. It is a cornerstone spice in Levantine cookery, appearing in marinades, meatballs, and rice dishes. The name reflects its taste rather than being a blend of spices.

content.text: Shish taouk is the everyday chicken kebab of the Levantine grill. Boneless thigh pieces (not breast, which turns fibrous over high heat) go into a marinade of yoghurt, lemon, garlic, tomato purée, and

for at least an hour, then thread onto skewers and cook over very high heat until charred at the edges and just cooked through. The yoghurt proteins denature in the heat and stick to the meat, creating a crust as the skewers sit over the grill. This is one of the few dishes where a hot griddle pan on the hob comes close to the charcoal result.

Boneless chicken thigh is the right cut. It has enough fat to stay juicy at high temperatures and takes the marinade without turning soggy. If you use breast, cut the cooking time by two minutes and don't leave any pink. The pieces should be 3–4 cm cubes, which is large enough to thread without falling apart and small enough to cook through without burning the outside.

The accompaniments are part of the dish. Toum, the whipped garlic sauce of Lebanese cooking, is the traditional partner — you can buy it in good delis, or make a simplified version by blending four cloves of garlic with lemon juice and neutral oil until emulsified. Flatbread, diced tomato, and shredded cos lettuce round the plate out.

items.prepNote: boneless, skinless, cut into 3–4 cm pieces

content.text: Combine the yoghurt, lemon juice, grated garlic, tomato purée, olive oil, allspice, cumin, paprika, and cinnamon in a large bowl with a generous pinch of salt. Add the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thigh pieces and turn to coat thoroughly. Cover and refrigerate for at least one hour; overnight is better.

If using wooden skewers, soak them in water for thirty minutes first. Thread four or five pieces of chicken onto each skewer, pressing them together so they hold. Get the griddle pan or grill as hot as it will go. Cook the skewers for five to six minutes on the first side without moving them, letting the crust form. Turn and cook for another four to five minutes until the chicken is cooked through with no trace of pink at the centre.
Rest the skewers for two minutes off the heat. Serve in flatbread or on a plate alongside garlic sauce, chopped tomato, sliced white cabbage or cos lettuce, and a wedge of lemon.

items.cause: Cooked too long, or breast was used instead of thigh.
items.fix: Switch to boneless thigh. Check the internal temperature at 10 minutes total cooking; 74°C is done.

items.symptom: The marinade burns before the chicken is cooked through
items.cause: The pan was not hot enough, causing the marinade to steam and then scorch rather than char cleanly.
items.fix: Get the pan hotter before the skewers go in. A very hot pan chars the outside quickly; a moderate-heat pan allows moisture to escape and the marinade to burn.

items.cause: Pieces were too small or cut inconsistently.
items.fix: Cut the chicken into even 3–4 cm pieces. Thread with the skewer through the widest part of each piece.

items.cause: Insufficient salt in the marinade, or too short a marinating time.
items.fix: Salt the marinade well — it should taste slightly overseasoned before the chicken goes in. Marinate for at least two hours.

content.text: Shish taouk is the Lebanese and Syrian answer to the Turkish şiş kebab, its name carrying the Turkish word for skewer and the Arabic word for chicken. It is the chicken dish of the Levantine street and restaurant grill, eaten from Beirut to Damascus in flatbread with garlic sauce and pickled turnips stained pink with beetroot juice. In the UK it is a staple of Lebanese and Middle Eastern restaurants; a home version made with good thigh meat is close to the restaurant result, which is not always the case with grilled dishes.
